claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children - Alex, who's 17, and Blake, who's 18. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have two children - Alex (17) and Blake (18).

To assess eligibility for Alex and Blake, I need to understand their current situations since they're both over 16. 

Let me start with **Alex (17)**: Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, college courses, or an approved training program)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in sixth form doing A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you! Alex being in sixth form doing A-levels means they're in approved education.

Now for **Blake (18)**: Is Blake currently in approved education or training, or have they left education/training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake has left education - they're not studying anymore.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Blake is 18 and has left education, that's an important detail.

Now I need to ask about your living situation: **Do both Alex and Blake live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live with me here in the UK. I'm in Leeds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Now I need to understand if you're currently the one receiving Child Benefit for them, or if this would be a new claim.

**Are you currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex and Blake, or would this be a new claim?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: This would be a new claim - I'm not currently claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Just to confirm a couple more details:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake** (for example, another parent or guardian)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. I have enough information to assess eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Based on what you've told me:

- **Alex (17)**: Lives with you in the UK and is in approved education (sixth form doing A-levels)
- **Blake (18)**: Lives with you in the UK but has left education

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

**For Alex (17): ✓ You ARE eligible for Child Benefit**
Alex is in approved education (sixth form doing A-levels) and is under 20, so you can claim Child Benefit for them.

**For Blake (18): ✗ You are NOT eligible for Child Benefit**
Since Blake has left education and is not in approved training, you cannot claim Child Benefit for them. Child Benefit continues after age 16 only if the young person stays in approved education or training, and only until they turn 20.

You can proceed to make a claim for Alex. If you have any questions about the application process or would like more information, please let me know!

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is currently in approved education (sixth form doing A-levels). Under Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, but you'll still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex meets these criteria - being under 20 and in approved education - and lives with you in the UK with no one else claiming for them, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 18 years old and has left education. Under Child Benefit rules, while you can normally claim for children under 20 if they stay in approved education or training, Blake is no longer in education or training. The rules state you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training, or starts an apprenticeship, or gets certain benefits. Since Blake has left education and is not in approved training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (17) who is in sixth form doing A-levels, and Blake (18) who has left education. Both live with the claimant in the UK (Leeds), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child. Alex qualifies because they are under 20 and in approved education. Blake does not qualify because they are over 16 and no longer in approved education or training.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and is currently in approved education (sixth form doing A-levels). Under Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, but you'll still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex meets these criteria - being under 20 and in approved education - and lives with you in the UK with no one else claiming for them, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 18 years old and has left education. Under Child Benefit rules, while you can normally claim for children under 20 if they stay in approved education or training, Blake is no longer in education or training. The rules state you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training, or starts an apprenticeship, or gets certain benefits. Since Blake has left education and is not in approved training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (17) who is in sixth form doing A-levels, and Blake (18) who has left education. Both live with the claimant in the UK (Leeds), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child. Alex qualifies because they are under 20 and in approved education. Blake does not qualify because they are over 16 and no longer in approved education or training.
